{"key_event":"Federal reconstruction amid al-Shabaab insurgency","reasoning":"From the 2000s collapse baseline, the 2012 federal government, provisional constitution, rebuilt ministries, federal member states, improved revenue/customs systems, and internationally backed security gains raised state capacity, but authority remained fragmented, externally dependent, and contested by al-Shabaab. Civil society gained some leverage through federal bargaining, diaspora/private networks, mobile money, and broader indirect elections, while famine, displacement, insecurity, vote-buying, and insurgent coercion kept autonomous civic power only moderate-low.","society_change":0.5,"society_power":4.8,"state_change":1.4,"state_power":3.2}
